Let me walk through the dangers of that in a broad audience. If you're not, if you're speaking to the needs that will get the attention of the initial person, but it will disqualify you with a decision-maker, you don't want to have any targeting for the decision-maker because you want them to share it with a person who needs to investigate it so that later it comes back to them for review. Like, it's no different than, you know, and this is a poor example, because it doesn't really happen, but it's kind of a conceptual example that I'm responsible for the Facebook advertising we do with our clients, but I'm not in Facebook every day. That's Lauren's job. So I might see something that's, you know, hey, this is the latest and greatest thing about Facebook and it's whatever and it's great. I can imagine a scenario where someone in my position might share that with Lauren to say, hey, check this out and then Lauren, look at it and later Lauren might come back to me and say, hey, that thing you sent to me actually looks really good. It's a thousand bucks, are you cool with me buying it? Yeah, sure, buy it, everything's great. Like, sure, conceptually I can imagine that happening, but if the thing, if the ad I saw spoke to Lauren in what would interest him and not to me, then that wouldn't happen or if it spoke to me, but not to Lauren, like the content didn't speak to him, then there's a mismatch. So it suggests to me that we want to have two parallel tracks where we want an environment like LinkedIn where you can target my job title and say, here's the messaging, either here's the messaging for the executive level, the EVP level, GM level, whatever it is, here's the messaging for them and what resonates most with them by industry and you can bifurcate it that way. And then here's the messaging for everybody in these other roles who considers it if it's not the same messaging. Like that's one way to approach it, just the challenge of LinkedIn, it's a small pool of high quality. So in Facebook it's much harder to do that job title. Lauren can speak to this, but I don't believe job title is broadly applicable on, broadly useful, as broadly useful on Facebook just because it's I know I don't have my job title on my Facebook. Yeah, right. So, I mean, is that, you're kind of following that logic there with that about how you approach this to show, I actually think, so if I woke up tomorrow only your business knowing what I know, this is the framework I look through things. I would start by focusing only on the people whose job it is to know about what you offer and have such a, and this is an idea borrowed from Seth Godin, but really emphasize the story that those people are going to be able to tell to the decision maker. And I think it's in, there's a Udemy course, Seth Godin has not the big one on the, not the condensed version of the marketing seminar, but even a smaller one. And I think it's about storing it. And if you want to email me, I can send you exactly which one it is. But one of the things he mentions in that is, you want to have a story about your offer for the person to tell other people. So if you're the safe solution, if you've been in business for a hundred years and nobody's ever gone wrong, hiring the company that's been in business a hundred years, then the story that the person who makes the recommendation to buy your stuff to the boss is able to tell is really the story about, I'm not going to get in any trouble recommending the industry leader that's been in business for a hundred years. No one's going to question that. It's a safe, that's a safe story. That may, that's not the only story, but you need to have a, you need to craft your messaging around the story that the person who you're targeting is going to be able to tell about you to the decision maker. So that story could be, this is, this seems to be the latest and greatest thing that's totally cutting edge. Or this is, it seems like everybody's talking about this. You name it, there are variations of that story, but that's actually, that's the messaging for that audience who then is going to tell the decision maker why to buy what you have to offer in language that you really can't know in advance because you don't know, like you don't know exactly what pushes the decision makers button. So you're selling, you're selling your story to the person who's going to bring it to the decision maker, who's going to inform their story from the benefits and testimonials and other things from your offer, but in their own language, in their own way, because you've given them a story to tell. That's a great point. Yes. Another thought that popped into my head as we were going through this is, I'm really going broad, and I'm thinking, I'm thinking it's sort of a, across all companies, horizontally across all companies, all levels. And I just realized that different people can see different campaigns, and I might be able to front load specific messaging that hits industry specific or at least segment specific and appeals to the people whose job it is to do the work and to find out the solution, but also the decision maker with enough specificity so that they don't, at least they get me on the phone. And if I work out multiple campaigns, my wherever the landing page is or wherever the website is could be generic information, but there's no reason why I can't target different segments specifically for both of those, both of the people. Because there'll be no confusion because one segment is not going to see the other segments ads and the second segment is not going to see the other segments ads. That might add a little bit, that might make it a bit easier on my end to solve the problem. And then it's just a prioritization. Stack the deck in your favor, where do you have the most compelling, where can you say the most compelling things, create the most compelling value, like stack everything in your favor. I like to, when I approach paid traffic in general, or, and it's not just limited to paid traffic, but it's just such a good microcosm. I just, my whole thing is how could I most stack the deck in my favor so that it should be an easy win? And it's interesting how hesitant people are to do that, but because our targeting is so granular on platforms like LinkedIn and others, that just stack the deck in your favor, speak to the felt needs of a smaller audience that where you really know that you can connect the dots there. And then, and if that, and this is a, one of the reasons this is so powerful is if that doesn't work, it should, right? I mean, where things get weird is if you do something really broad and it doesn't work and you don't really know, first of all, you don't know, is it never going to work? Can I iterate my way to success? Or it just lots of questions, like what did I, what have I done wrong? And it's so hard to figure it out. When you do a narrower audience with a much narrower focus that should be a nice fat pitch over the plate that should just work. And if it doesn't, then I have a sec. Well, then you have a small window which to look for the issues. Like, is it a messaging issue? Did I, like, what did I get wrong? And you can iterate out of that box. That's a great feeling. Much faster, right? That's so much faster. Yeah. Cool. Thanks Kyle. I appreciate it. Yeah, I appreciate it. That's, it's very insightful. It's also helps that the industry that I'm targeting is huge. So it's my expertise. So it's, it's, I can, I can put together a couple of fat pitches and get some instant feedback. But I, thank you very much for your help. That helps me clarify my thinking really, it really helps to talk to somebody. Thank you. Yeah, absolutely. And thank you Kyle. And if you have questions, you know, follow up stuff that bubbles up, shoot an email to the, and just reply to the Q and A email or whatever that gets to me. And we can just keep the conversation going. Thank you. Yeah, thanks Kyle. All right. Let's, Francis asked a couple of great questions. So I'll answer the last one, the quick one. As Agile the same as Scrum, Scrum is a method, it's an Agile methodology. So if Scrum is Agile, but Agile is not necessarily Scrum, it's just one flavor of Scrum. Kanban is a flavor of Agile, Scrum, there are others, get more detailed, more specific and sort of more, there are different reasons to do different things at different team sizes and different applications. But Agile as a philosophy and principle is exceptionally powerful. At the principle level, it's exceptionally powerful because it's, it focuses on the rate at which work flows through a system and how to make that flow faster. And that simple organizing principle has so many downstream consequences that if you just internalize that simple principle, which incidentally applies to everything that we do in marketing is that we are trying to increase the flow, the throughput through the system we've created for our business. So there's a direct parallel there. So the more you know about Agile, there's direct immediate crossover to your business in general. Francis asked one of my favorite questions, which is I love to ask people, if I have 10 minutes hallway with somebody who's a subject matter expert in something, I'll often ask them like, what are the three most common mistakes you see people who whatever you fill in the blank do? Or what are three things that you've learned over the last 20 years working with X that you think are most important? I love questions like this. So Francis's question, what are the top three avoidable mistakes you've made? That's a great question. So I will, you know, I probably have 3000. So I think that's why my brain is on over a job trying to figure out what the three mistakes are. The probably the biggest mistake I made early, and I remember the moment where the epiphany happened, I don't know exactly where it was in my office, I don't know everything about it. I may have mentioned this before, so I apologize if I'm repeating here, but this was just so profound to me that it continues to be profound. But the, I thought, I was not a born entrepreneur. I, this happened to me sort of just because of a lot of things that happened in my life. My father was diagnosed with cancer, I moved home, I had a different career, different profession, never expected to have my own business ever. And then I just woke up one day and decided I didn't like, you know, I needed to be able to eat. So that was just, it was the logical outcome of a set of decisions I had to make. So I went, I was instantly in business and needed to be profitable in month one. So I don't have, I didn't have a background of what to do, what to expect, which I'm very thankful for because I think a lot of conventional business advice is worthless. And I am a adjunct professor of entrepreneurship and a lot of what I teach in the classroom is how worthless most business advice is. So the first mistake that I made early was thinking that there was a point at which I was going to have quote, made it, right? So that I was trying to reach a point and it was infinitely frustrating. Is I would, I would try to, like I would, I always had this idea that there was a mountain I was climbing and once I got to the top of that mountain, I could relax. And I was reading, I believe it was the obstacle is the way or I think that's the Ryan Holiday book where they had, he quotes, there's a Haitian proverb that behind mountains are more mountains. And the point of that is you're not going, in business there is no making it, right? I think I mentioned this in an email recently, like, you know, Warren Buffett's in his late 80s, mid 80s, goes to work every day, you know, Jeff Bezos, worth however many, Deca Billionaire. And you're to put a, we hear billion, now everyone's talking about billionaires and it's kind of lost its impact on us. You know, the stat that shocks me is, you know, a million seconds is like four and a half days, a billion seconds is 33 and a half years. That's the difference between a million and a billion. And Jeff Bezos has tens of billions, he's worth tens of billions of dollars. And what does he do? He goes to work every day, right? So this realization that you have to fall in love with the game, you're not trying to get somewhere. You know, when Andre and I have written about this when we talk about this, the infinite game, it never ends, like it's that. But it's also this recognition that all of the juice of work happens in the act of working. Like that, that's where it all is. That's where all, like all of it happens. And because I was often looking ahead, trying to get to this point that is not achievable, it was a huge mistake, you know, when I go back and tell myself not to make it, I don't know. I mean, I'm glad that I made it because it was such a profound realization that every day is a chance to play the game and I love the game. So every day is a win. I get to win every single day and feel the benefits of that winning. So that's sort of a dual thing, it's a mistake. And you see that I have not to make the mistake sort of packaged into one. Other avoidable mistakes that I've made, I have, this was more a long time ago and I've probably overcorrected for it, but I have had, and this again, be careful of the context here. I'm telling you something that I do now have you reached a level of success, not what I did to get here. So be very careful about that kind of advice. But I did let my ego get in the way a couple of times. I remember being pissed off about a few things and you're getting wound up with clients about some stuff that it was unnecessary and it was foolish, it was ego. Yeah, anytime my ego shows up to the party, it's not great. Now be careful with that one though, because ego also, it was a big driver for me and ego pushed me to do a lot of the work. So, is that a mistake? If it would be a mistake now, I mean, something I'm very proud of now is I really don't bring a lot of ego to the party. I bring confidence. There are things I've accomplished that I'm proud of, but I don't believe I have the only answers or the canonical answers. I just, one person's opinion, based on a set of experience and I offer it that way and with no expectation of its seat. Like it's just, I don't have ego now, but that's 25 years into the game. So keep that one with a grain of salt from your own perspective. Nothing wrong with ego. If ego is directed in a way that's healthy, ego just has this funny way of perpetuating itself. It's where it exists to perpetuate itself. I think the biggest mistake that I made, and again, I wanna be cautious with this one because this is dramatically affected by context, but I tried to do everything myself for a really, really long time, just 20 plus years. And when I say myself, I've been blessed to work with people like Lauren and others who were gifted and I don't mean I did all the work, but ultimately I took on 100% responsibility and that's how I'm wired. Agency is very important to me. I have an internal locus of control. I believe it is up to me to happen to the world, not for the world to happen to me. So there's, because of that, I think I over-emphasized my responsibility and having had an experience for the last five-ish months or so of a true partnership with a dear friend and my most capable thought partner. Like the things that I'm able to accomplish working with Andre as a collaborative partner are not different in degree, like a little bit better. It's a difference in kind. The whole experience is fundamentally different. Now, I don't know that you can just walk down the street and find, Andre and I tend to finish each other's sentences and it's freaky how we think alike and we're able to write together in ways that don't make sense because, so there's a lot there that, I recognize it's a little bit, there's some weirdness there, but I think the underlying mistake that was the precursor to that was that I didn't, like I did too much of it myself, I relied too much on myself and didn't find help where help was available beyond some smaller areas. So, that's a mistake that I, looking back, that feels like a mistake to me. The more I interact with others and get that recognition of the juice from it, like that's really, really exciting to me and my creative output, working in collaboration with a thought partner is so much different than what I create on my own, that it's not recognizable as even being the same act, which is amazing to me. And your second question on this, Fought three mistakes about getting clients, that's a great question too. I think my biggest mistake, maybe, might talk myself out of this, my biggest client getting mistake, probably was never trying to get clients. That sounds so ridiculous, and this is an example of me to a fault. Like I just, and I still think of not this, I've chased one client in my entire, I don't know, 21 years running an agency, meaning I actively went after one client and it was a disaster and it felt so gross. And it's a client who it should have been easy. They're at the local company, I love their product. I knew it backwards and forth. I could have been a salesperson for them. I could have sold the hell out of that and done it exactly right. And I reached out to a friend, it was a long time client, my first client who I still have, and he introduced me to the owner and it was a disaster and I felt gross the whole time. Like I was chasing a client. So it's just so funny to think how terribly that warped me. So, not, I relied on, I relied on 100% pull toward me through doing good work, which is great. I'm very proud of that, but it also had its own downside that I did not have a system with a dial when there were times when, for whatever reason, work wasn't coming in well, or like I never had the security of a system that was producing results for me. So if I could go back, knowing what I know now, I would have built that system early and I would have built that system around, at least exposing me to good clients and then having the opportunity to talk to them. I relied entirely on relationships for 21 years to get clients. I'm exceptionally lucky that it worked as well as it did, but that was a real mistake. Don't make that mistake. Build your client acquisition system for your, if you work with clients, build that system and care for that system and feed that system and protect it. It's very important. There's nothing wrong with it. It was my, it was stupidity on my part not to do that. Now the path that I took, I'm glad I, in many ways I took it, I emphasized relationships, but I would have loved the peace of mind of having a dial and some of the times when I didn't and it sucked. There were times that it was awful and scary and I don't wish that on anyone. I think a second mistake, a client getting a mistake, I think you have a particular example. So I wanna be cautious with this one. This is a, I'm not nearly as emphatic about this one as the one I just answered, but this is more of a, there was one client I always wanted from day one for a variety of reasons. It was an area of interest of mine. I had a lot of respect for the client, the person who owned the business or the personality of the business, like all of that. And I eventually got the client and it was one of the worst clients I've ever had. And he was a truly miserable human being, an unhappy and an unkind. There's a big company. The entire company took on his personality. It was gross, it was an awful experience. So I got, I think the mistake I made there was thinking that this public persona of a person who seemed likable and his business, I was really interested in that somehow that translated to a good client. The underlying mistake there is that I didn't do the work at the time I've done it since to really know the ingredients of a great client for me. And I know those now, and I don't work with clients to not taking on new clients now, but when in the last few years, when I've been very aware of this, I've taken on clients that have met those criteria. And that has made an enormous difference, not just getting blinded by sunshine unicorns and taking on a client that I thought was gonna be amazing, who there was horrendous, horrendous experience. I'm tempted to say for the third mistake is go back and see the first mistake. Like that's so powerful. So again, let me re-emphasize, build a system that produces the opportunity to get new clients, right? Not, I guess that's a good segue into the last mistake. I've worked with clients I shouldn't have. I've taken clients because of the money, which is really an extension of the first problem is I didn't have a system that produced really high quality opportunity. So there was a period of time where I worked with a series of clients back to back all from a single referral partner who was a terrible fit. Everything about the situation said I shouldn't do the work. Yet I did the work and I did it entirely for financial reasons. In many ways, I'm glad it happened because I can look back on that and it was such a teaching lesson. So I wouldn't want to give that up, but wisdom is learning from the mistakes of others. So here's your opportunity for some wisdom from today's call. There is no upside to working with bad clients. Having just given you some upside of saying that's a great learning experience. For you, now that I can tell you categorically how awful it is, there's no upside for you to have to go through the experience yourself. And don't take on the experience intellectually. That's not enough. What you really need to do to prevent this mistake, Josh Weitzkin calls this fire walking. You need to burn it in physiologically. You need to feel what it feels like to work with awful clients or to work with clients who you don't, it's a bad fit, you shouldn't work them. They're not paying you enough. They're like, they don't know enough, whatever it is. Like you need to sit with that, burn it in, really think about what it's like to get dumb questions over and over again, to feel like whatever it is for you, burn it in so that you avoid it habitually, that you don't let it happen to you. And the way to prevent the mechanism to make it so that you don't need to have it happen is to build a system that's going to consistently, produce a big pool of people who you can say no to. And within that big pool of people who you can say no to are going to be two or three gems consistently that you can say yes to. That's a really important system to have in place. Don't try to build a client acquisition system where every time it spits out an opportunity from the end, you think you're taking the opportunity. It spits out opportunities. Design whatever your front end system is. If put the bait out for the fish that you want to catch, if you want a certain type of client, then how you position yourself needs to attract that kind of client. And yes, you'll get some other lower value ones or you'll get other stuff in there, but you want to get enough that you can say no to six, seven, eight out of 10 of them. Nine out of 10 of them, 19 out of 20 of them, whatever it is, as long as the economics are there, there are an infinite number of potential clients for you out there. Don't worry about that. That's another mistake we seem to add here, like 3A. Don't think that they're, and maybe for your business, you can look at, I only serve 12 big companies. Well, sure, there are limits, but the limit to the value you can create and the expertise that you can bring and the people who can benefit from that is effectively limitless. You're not going to run out. So build a system that attracts people to your world who are going to, some percentage of them are going to be ideal and focus on the ideal. Don't let yourself get sucked into the, particularly the ones that are just clearly not fits. It's a long answer, too long answers. Thank you, Francis. Those are great questions. Performance profit sharing, infinitely easier to get than equity in general. Equity, you're getting literally a part of the business and from the perspective of the shares of their 10,000 shares and you get 1,000 of them, you own 10% of the business. That's just sort of how it works and you get the benefits of 10% of that business. So equity is much harder. It's a much harder conversation to have. I think that the advice I would give you here, I wanna give you simple actionable advice, not, my goal here is to give you simple actionable advice that I think is actually a contribution I can make not to tell you things you've probably already thought about or that other people would answer better around the mechanics of it. I have performance-based relationships with clients in the past. I had an equity relationship that I left that business because the other people involved just couldn't find their ass with both hands. So I've been involved in different variations of this, but here's the insight that I wanna leave you with on this. You have a presupposition in your thinking that is costing you and that presupposition is that it's up to you to find the solution and it's not. This is a collaboration and the best person to collaborate within my experience and the way to get way more than you might offer yourself is to ask the other party, have them solve the challenge for you. So what I would do in this situation is I would make this case that you're sort of as you've made it, I would set up a conversation about only this topic. But then have scheduled a conversation with this client or whatever the decision makers are, whoever really you feel like that, the appropriate people have this conversation. And what you want to do is you want to show them the future that you're imagining. So you talk about the successes you've had and then really, and be open and honest and candid about, like I feel like we're scratching the surface here. We've done all of these things already, terrible environment to try to be doing anything. We know things are gonna get easier and better. Position yourself as the expert, command their respect, not demand it. So you're commanding their respect through your expertise by showing them sort of a high level picture of what's happened so far, but then also showing where you think this could go and show your work a little bit. You don't have to get in detail. This is not a detailed conversation, but it's not a, we've done X and I think 20 times X is possible because it's not believable. We've done X and we think 20 times X is possible because of these five factors and that may be a little conservative or whatever. I mean, but show your work a little bit. Show your thinking. Here's where we are, here's where we're going. Here's where I think we could be going. Here's what's involved. And then you want to bring your, you wanna go first, essentially. And going first is you saying, the more, essentially saying, the more we've been involved with this project, the more I've realized that this is a perfect area for us to demonstrate our expertise. I've really enjoyed working with your team. It like really speak from the heart. Don't make this shit up. Speak from the heart, be honest. Here's why I think this really is a synergistic relationship. Maybe don't use synergy, whatever. That's the definition of synergy. It's just, you've spelled it out. Make that case and then make it clear what you want. Say, and you kind of wrap it up into that. Like when I look at, when I see all of this information together, it leads me to this conclusion that I would really love to be more involved. And I would, I really want, I or we, if you're your team, we really want to be part of what happens next. But to do that, and then I would, I'm not in a but, I'm making an and, say, and to do that, I would like to work with you to develop a compensation model that makes sense for everyone, where everybody wins when we succeed together. Like that sets the conceptual foundation. Now it's not you asking something from him or her or you're no longer facing each other across the table in a way that's low level adversarial. It's two parties, not, not, you know, mean adversarial, but it's two parties looking at each other. Now you've reoriented to it's your side by side looking out on the world together. And really have that mindset. Imagine that. This is a great, by the way, if you can do this, especially with men, if men can, if you can have these conversations in a car, it's the greatest thing in the world because there's something, I don't know the psychology about it. I know people study this, but there's something about men, and this is not true for women. Women tend to communicate with each other, facing each other, men for some reason, when we face each other, that's more likely to be interpreted as aggression. This is again, this is not my research, this is somebody else's, but that idea of shoulder to shoulder when you're facing the same direction, particularly effective with two men. I've seen this happen over and over again in my professional experience, but you want that mindset. How do you literally or conceptually change the dynamic? So it's you and the other party looking out at the opportunity together. How would you together define a compensation model that makes sense for everyone? Now you're distributing the responsibility for that, and then the other person gets to, and you might lead with a couple of ideas. They don't want you disappearing as much as you don't want to disappear. And that's important to understand too, is they want you as much as you want them because you're creating results. You're not looking at the opportunity from different sides of a table, trying to figure out, because then it becomes, you know, what are they getting, what are you getting, that you don't want that, you want what are we getting, make it we. I've been business partners with two other people in a business that was never going to go anywhere and I should have seen it sooner and I wasted way too much time, way too much emotional energy. I've also had a business partner where I chose the business partner and it was absolutely disastrous and it was disastrous because for a variety of reasons it was one of my best friends who was really no longer even a friend. We don't speak at all, ruined a 30 year, I suspect ruined a 30 year relationship. There were infinite number of lessons in there. It was an evaluation on something that should have so obviously worked because of the complementary skill sets and what I brought to the party and what the other person brought to the party, like it on paper, everything about it should have worked and because it should have worked, both of us were willing to make assumptions about how it would work and we were not, we didn't do the hard work of sitting down and talking about what would happen when we would disagree and we just didn't do that work. So whatever, I mentioned that, that whatever you, if you do choose a business partner that don't skip over that work. And there are rare exceptions, which I guess I all mentioned also. If I had to go pick a business partner, so and I guess the third example here, the obvious example, right? If you, and this is the semi-tongue and cheek example, but there is wisdom in it. So let me kind of do the funny ha ha part and then tell you where I think is the wisdom in it. So the funny part, someone asked me today a similar question, it was like, well, you just need to find somebody who thinks exactly like you do, values everything that you value, works exactly the same way that you work, is very tolerant of your nonsense and you're tolerant of their nonsense. At the end of the day, they're just somebody you genuinely like and hang out with. So like, if I woke up tomorrow and Jeff Bezos decided that one of those billions was just getting in his way and he said, Sean, I know you've been a customer for a long time. I know you had a lot of books that you haven't read and that damn Kindle that you keep stacking up. So here's a billion dollars all the time in the world now, you can go finish all those books. What would you do? I would continue to figure out ways that I could work with my business partner, Andre. And most likely doing very similar things because I have been very fortunate to have stumbled on something that I didn't even, I wouldn't have believed existed in that collaborative partnership. So I'm exceptionally lucky in there, but in there, the wisdom is in the ingredients. Ingredient number one, I would be friends with Andre and was before this would be after this and in it. That's sort of the first critical ingredient. I like spending time with him, I like thinking with him, I like him as a person, I like to hang out with him. So that's a very, if you have a business partner, you're gonna spend a lot of time together, you need to like the person. If you think you can tolerate a business partner because they produce results, you are on the fast track to hell because it's done, you have to like the other person. And I think really liking them helps. I think you have to, the next most important thing that's probably part of liking someone is you have to have congruent values. Andre and I in very weird ways, our values are very similar, eerily similar, that's the weird part, where we care about things that are outside the norm, higher than, it's just, it's weird. Example, I think we've shared publicly before, maybe in emails, at the end of the traffic engine, the last email that went out, I had an idea that from a marketing perspective was bat shit crazy. And I sent Andre the email, I think it ended Monday, the bat shit crazy idea was, let's discourage people from buying on the last day by giving them effectively an eight week free course of great material and like putting it together. From a conversion standpoint, from a last day of a promotion standpoint, that is so bat shit crazy that 999 out of 1,000 marketers who do what we do in that same situation would have looked at me like I had nine heads. And there certainly was a part of me that was like, I think Andre's gonna think I'm crazy. And as soon as I said it, his response was like immediate, like, oh hell yeah, that sounds great, let's do that. And we've since done it again. That's weird, right? That's a congruence of values that we both overvalue the customer experience relative than squeezing out a few more dollars. That's rare and it doesn't have to be that value, but your values absolutely have to align because values are a place where we don't have a lot of give. And that's, you can give on methods, there's lots where you have some buffer. We don't have a lot of value, you don't have a lot of buffer and values. If somebody violates your values, you feel it instantly and viscerally, you still like it. So I think that values is a core thing. And then I think a last thing with a business partner, this is probably an extension of liking the other person, it's probably an extension of similar values and other things, but there is a component of this that is a willingness to, well, I'll give you two more things. This one and there's another one. You have to have a willingness to defer the individual decisions to the decisions of both of you. And then I'm not explaining this well, so I'll give it to you as an example. Andre and I are thinking about doing something that I'm really excited about. And I thought to put words in Andre's mouth, but I think I can say that he's more reserved about than I am. And I'm very sensitive to that. And I'm 100% present and comfortable with the fact that I'm really excited about it, but I'm also as present and as comfortable with the fact that he has more reservations about doing it. And I've said before, repeatedly, the analogy I use is, I like to jump out of airplanes and make parachutes before I hit the ground. I'm comfortable doing that. But I also know that's not necessarily his style in some ways. So the mechanism that has come out of that that I think is critical for a good partnership is, I know in my mind that on a scale of one to 10, my enthusiasm for this project is high, but it's lower than his reservations about the project. So let's just say on a scale of one to 10 that my enthusiasm is an eight and his reservation is a nine. There are two ways to approach this. I could try to coerce him. I could do a lot of different, there's a lot I could do to try to move him, but I know in my heart that there's reservations. So to have that model, and it's just a framework for the way I think about it, but to have that model allows me to look at that and say, I can be 100% okay that I'm excited about this. And I can also recognize that my excitement doesn't rise to the level of his reservation. And I'm going to let his reservation be the thing that drives the decision making and feel good about that. I don't feel slighted. I don't feel like I'm not getting something that I want. Damn it, why can I talk him into it? I feel I have so much peace with it because of just thinking about that framework. And I think, I don't think that is something that, I know that he would respect, and I think a part of it is it gets back to values. I know he respects that, that if the roles were reversed, I have this strong suspicion that if he was kind of mildly uncomfortable and knew I was over the top excited, that the roles would reverse. So it makes it very easy. So having some kind of framework like that. And the last part that I just hinted at a second ago, if you can work with somebody where the work you do is better than what you do on your own in a way that you don't understand, and that it's easier in ways that you don't understand, and that you see the output of that work and are genuinely impressed by it, but with no ego, like you don't feel like you, you owned it, you made it. Andre and I write together a lot. And sometimes I can see what he wrote and what I wrote, but it doesn't feel like that. We create, and it's better than what I create. And I think, again, putting words in his mouth, that what we create together is better than what he creates on his own too. So if you find that, and it may not, it doesn't have to be a creative collaboration in the sense of your producing video or written word, whatever, you could be making a business and you and your partner could have complimentary skill sets so that the thing that you make as a result of that partnership is greater than what either of you could make on your own, that to me is the very definition of a great partnership. And it can happen in different levels too. Like, Lauren and I are not business partners, but we're very much intellectual partners in this endeavor that we work on together. And it has a similar dynamic that I believe what happens as a result of the work we do together is better than what Lauren produces or what I produce on my own. Certainly better than what I would produce on my own. I know that. You can think of partnership broadly speaking. I think of people who work with me in any capacity as partners in some capacity and partners in this work. So very long answer to a short question, but a beautiful question. I think really the most important question, one of the most important questions we can ask is who out there could I do this with? Because we're social creatures. Even me, I love to lock myself in my office and I have my own weirdness around that and I like to be alone and I like introspection. But I'm also a social creature. I like there are people I like to be around. And when you find that vibe, it's electric and you want more of it and the experience changes. And when you get it right, it's the most beautiful thing you can imagine. And when you get it wrong, it's pretty ugly. So stay away from the wrong stuff. Blimey flash of the obvious. All right, great questions, Francis. Thank you. Last question, Lauren, this is going to be, this is the two of us. This is a hard one too, I'm gonna have you answer this. Sorry, I'm gonna have you answer this and then I'll chime in. This is from Gareth as well. This is a Facebook ad question. Gareth says, I've created a 900 word advertisement on Facebook and all the metrics were great when analyzed through Lauren's process. Only thing that's concerning is the lack of signups. So everything's great except the only thing that matters. Gotcha. All right, I've generated 85 visitors and zero signups. I've never had that in my life. I've previewed the ads, clicked the link and it works. I don't believe there's a technical reason. I've created a split test page with a very simple landing page that repeats the final paragraphs called action. So there are no surprises and still no signups. And Lauren's right up, he says that something people could be clicking through from the learn more button without clicking to read more and fully pre-qualifying. But I'm gonna lost to understand how I can send 85 plus people and not get a signup. Any idea what I could be doing so drastically wrong? Lauren? Well, I did notice he said that he checked the tech, which as I've mentioned before, anytime I'm seeing a zero, I just wanna reassure myself that everything's functionally working. If it is, and he says he's checked it, then it gets difficult. What I like to do is I like to set up, I like to kind of envision the flow of someone through the process. So they see the ad and then they click on see more. Then they perhaps click over to the landing page. From the landing page, maybe they spend a certain amount of time on a landing page. Then from that landing page, let's say it's a multi-page pre-sell, okay? So then they go to page two, then they go to page three, then they opt in. Then they visit a sales page, they add to cart, they end up purchasing. However long your process is, I like to get numbers on each step in that process. Particularly as I think we covered in the modules, there's ways that you can, with Google Tag Manager, you can add, you can fire a pixel code on a page after someone has been on the page for a certain amount of time, 10 seconds, 15 seconds, something like that. For any page where you are hoping for them to read like page one of a multi-page pre-sell, or all the pages of multi-page pre-sell for a long copy landing page, you can add those tags in so that you can see, okay, of the people who are visiting that landing page, how many people are actually sticking around long enough that they're hopefully consuming the content. And then you just take it from point one, all the way to the conclusion, and see where does a breakdown occur? Now, you say that your metrics are good, so I'm guessing you're looking at your CTRL, which is what you would look at for that first step. That's showing you how many people are having any engagement on your ad. Typically, that's people clicking on see more. Then you're looking at your CTRL, which is how many people are clicking your Facebook ad and then visiting your landing page. Those metrics you're saying are good, then the problem is, okay, probably not there in terms of, well, at least generating clicks. Then you go to the landing page. Your one thing I would look at is actually calculating of the people who click how many become landing page views. In the dashboard, there's a way to select landing page views. I don't think it comes up on any of the kind of preset dashboards. You might need to customize the columns and add it in. But look at landing page views. Now, what a landing page view is, is it's when the Facebook pixel loads and you spend some amount of time on there, a fraction of a second. I don't know exactly when Facebook counts it as a landing page view, but it's somewhat, it's basically after the page is loaded and the pixel is actually fired. And you would be surprised how many people drop off because of a slow loading page, particularly since many people are viewing it on mobile, they may have poor connections. You'd be surprised how many people will click your link and drop off before the page even loads. If you get above 85%, that's good. That's where I would expect it to be. Above 90 is actually very good. I wanted to see above 85. If you're seeing 60%, 50%, well, then you know, okay, I need to work on my page load speed. Take a look and see if the click to landing page ratio is good, if it's right around 85% or higher. Then it sounds like you've got a short page, so I wouldn't worry about putting any tags to fire, to see how much people, how much time people are spending on it to see if there's some break off there. But what you're getting is you're just not getting people signing up. So it sounds like you're going like a one page sign up and they sign up right there. In that case, what you need to do is whenever you have a breakdown, you know it's not after that, right? Obviously, you don't need to worry about stuff after that because you're not getting sign ups. There may be problems further down the line, but that's not your constraint at the moment. So why are you not getting sign ups? Well, it's due to something before that. And that's where a lot of the art comes into play. You said that you're getting good metrics on your Facebook ad, okay good. So I look at the data on each step, but then you look at the metrics, okay? But once you have a breakdown that your data reveals, then you need to look further up the stream to say where, what is leading to this breakdown? Because just because you have a breakdown at the level of getting sign ups doesn't mean that's where the problem is. The problem may be way, way, way back on step one or step two. And that's where a lot of art comes into it. Take a look at, is it the messaging? Is it that the ad is not congruent? Is the image, if you have a very like hype image that people are clicking on and not even reading your copy, they're just immediately clicking over to your landing page. And then your landing page is just really short and sweet. People will be like, why am I opting for this? What's the big deal for this? So there is a lot of art to it, unfortunately. I mean, I wish I could give you some conclusive look here, but some of the places I would look, if you look at the ratio between your CTR all and your CTR, if you're, I wanna see two or three to one, somewhere around there is good. Higher than that, five to one, six to one, that's okay. As long as you're getting a good CTR, that just means that people are clicking C more, reading your copy and not clicking over. That, as long as you're getting a good CTR, then that's probably not a huge issue. If you're seeing it closer to one to one, if your CTR all is say, 3% and your CTR is 3%, then what that tells you is people aren't even clicking on C more, they're just clicking over. So take a look at that and then say, okay, do I need to tweak my image? It could be that your copy, if people are clicking, then your copy's doing a good job of driving clicks, but is it properly setting the stage? Is it getting people, is it congruent with the experience that people are getting on the landing page? And then are you selling the sign up or are you just driving the click? You could be very curiosity based, just take that next step, or are you selling the benefits of what they're gonna get in the sign up? I imagine if you've read what Sean's written so far in the traffic engine, you're doing a good job of selling. Then take a look at your landing page. Is it, again, is it kind of congruent with what you've set in the ad? Is there some disconnect there? Is there any technical issue, like slow, low time? Or perhaps maybe try a different landing page, maybe add some more copy into it other than that brief little line. See if that matters. Really is the type thing where you kind of take a look at it, maybe have an experienced person, experienced eye look at it and go with your gut. I'll add one technical thing and one conceptual thing, which I think is just a variation of what you said Lauren. So I'll say what you said and then take credit for it. It's my favorite thing to do. So from a technical perspective, make sure you're testing on mobile and testing a desktop. I've seen more times and I can count somebody goes through everything and just a problem and they do the tech on their desktop, everything seems to work and then you open it up on your phone and the opt-in box doesn't appear or some random weirdness like that. So rule out, you know, you can have a very high percentage of mobile users and some weird funkiness with mobile. Rule that out. Similar thing that Lauren mentioned, it's possible, especially in a long format, it's possible to ask a question in the ad that's answered on the landing page and it doesn't require opt-in and what they want is the answer to the question. And that's, I don't mean you actually ask a question, you can imply it, but you can open a loop in an ad and the loop, and that's strong enough that somebody clicks through the ad because they want to close that loop and then you close it on the landing page and it could be something as simple as like, what is this thing? Like, oh, it requires an opt-in, it's a thing, it's a download, it's not a video, or whatever, it could be something as simple functionally as that. Like they just wanna know like, what is this thing? I'm excited, I'm engaged, oh shit, it's an opt-in, forget it. Like it could be that or it could be you've hinted at some question or some, you've like Agora Financial is famous for this, I think I used some of their examples in one of the modules for the traffic engine where they talk about Obama's mistress, you're like, oh, what are they talking about? And Obama's mistress was the Fed, the US Federal Reserve, it was like, that was the reveal. So once you know that the hook, once you know what it is, you've closed that loop, you've got the dopamine shot, you're not pulled toward it anymore, and then you can bail and you don't have to worry about it. So I'm not imagining that your ad referenced Obama's mistress for the Federal Reserve and then you closed the loop that way, but that's a really demonstrative way to think about how very subtly we can do that. We can introduce, we can open loops and introduce some kind of question in the prospect's mind and want to give them value by answering it before the opt-in, but because we do that, we create a false signal because what we've really done is we've introduced a very subtle form of curiosity. And that's why curiosity is a tough one to use well. You've got to really know how to use curiosity for this very reason that you can get a lot of people who are just, they need to close the loop to get the square root of dopamine to know what the thing was from the ad, and then they can say, okay, great, oh, it's a course, or oh, I see, I'm gonna do this thing, I'm not gonna do that. So it's better, I've said this before, I don't know if I've said this yet in the traffic engine, but I think from a 10,000 foot perspective that when you open a loop in an ad, especially long form copy, you can open and close loops a lot, 900 words of copy. That if you open a loop in the ad, close it in the ad, don't close it on the landing page, the only loop to leave open in the ad needs to be one that's closed with the purchase of the product, or that's closed with the, whatever the next action is, if you wanna make it a little bit closer. So, but don't, in the past, it made sense in a low-cost environment to open loops and ads that were closed on landing pages because the economics worked, the economics don't work anymore to do that. So if you're going to leave something open, that's going to keep pulling a prospect forward, it needs to pull them forward to a deeper point, not just to the landing page, where you're just paying for curiosity clicks. So not suggesting that's what you did here, but those are two places I would look just for possibilities. One other thought I had, and this was something I realized I didn't mention in the previous Q&A call that we did, the core Q&A call, CCPA, I'll explain that in a minute, but CCPA may be rearing its ugly head. When you say that you're not getting any signups, I would actually look in whatever email CRM you're using and perhaps verify there, are you seeing signups there? Even if that means that you have a specific signup that is strictly for paid traffic and another one that's organic, so that you know that it's coming from paid traffic. When I say check your tech, don't just check to make sure that the signup goes through and that you receive the email. Also make sure the pixels are firing, because if you're seeing zero signups within Facebook, it could be that the pixel is not properly firing, that you're actually getting signups, but the pixel event is not just happening. So make sure that the pixel event is firing. To do that, you can look at Pixel Helper, I think is what it's called. I use it gosh so much, I don't even think about it anymore. It's actually a Chrome extension that Facebook has authored, so it's legit, it's not anything black hat. And it will tell you the Facebook pixel data that's firing, so that you'll be able to see it that way. Also within the Facebook ads manager side, you can go in there and test events and run through the funnel and make sure that the pixel events are firing properly. You can do that back in the events manager part of ads manager. Even if you're seeing that everything is happening, the pixel events are properly firing, what could be happening is that if any of that activity is coming from California, there's this new law that has been passed, the CCPA, I guess it's the California Consumer Privacy Act, I think is what it is or something. That has kind of thrown a little, the Facebook world into a tizzy a little bit, but basically the long and short of it is that for a resident of California, your Facebook events might fire, but then they do not get tracked in Facebook. In other words, your tech is set up perfectly, but if a sign up comes from California, then that will not register in Facebook. Anything that happens on the platform, if they click like, if they comment, if they click on an ad, any on-platform activity, if they share, on-platform activity is properly tracked in California, but anything on a website, so once they click over to your landing page and now they're on off-platform, they're on your website and the pixel events are firing there, even if you've done everything correctly and the pixel events are firing correctly, they may not be tracked properly in Facebook if that resident is from California. So what I would do is, CCPA probably does not apply to you. It actually applies to very few businesses. A business is making, I think, 25 million or more in annual revenue. There's a few other qualifications to see if CCPA applies to you. It probably doesn't. So what you can do is actually go within Facebook and turn off that CCPA feature. It's called like a limited data tag or something. I can't remember off the top of my head, but it's something you only have to do once. You just go in there and you tell Facebook, yeah, we don't need limited data usage because CCPA does not apply to us. I've talked to Facebook reps and Facebook's official point of view is that they're gonna provide you the tools to be compliant with CCPA if you decide that you need to. But they're not gonna advise you on whether or not you are, that the CCPA applies to you. They're not gonna advise you on the legality of that. They've encouraged everyone to reach out to their own legal teams. And then they're just gonna provide you the tools if you want to use them. What Facebook did was starting in July, they actually had that limited data use on by default to kind of, I guess, ease everyone into it. I don't really know why they did it that way, but that's the way it happened. So many people unaware that they're being treated as if the CCPA applies to them, even when it doesn't, because Facebook is doing it by default. All you have to do is go back into your ads manager somewhere. I, for the life of me, I can't remember. I had to do some searching to find it. And it's only something you have to do once. You just toggle off a little switch that says, yeah, I don't need to worry about limited data use because CCPA does not apply to me. In the coming weeks, I'm not exactly sure what Facebook is gonna do by default. Once you toggle that off, you're probably good to go. So that is something additional to check with the tech, to kind of summarize. Make sure that you're looking your CRM, make sure that you're actually getting or not getting emails, even if it works. And you're not seeing anything in Facebook dashboard. Then make sure that the Pixel events are firing properly and finally check to see if you've got that CCPA toggled on. One last thing, Gareth. All of this, of course, may not apply to your specific example. So send me an email with a link to the ad and a link to the landing page or pages. Take a look. If there's some, just some weirdness in it, we can take a look and maybe give you some advice on that. If it's more of a content thing. TARDOM, it's, you know, all of these things, we're sort of going through lots of different permutations of answers to be broadly useful for anyone listening to the recording of the call or reading the transcript, knowing that, you know, in your particular situation, it's probably not necessarily going to be one of those things. It's probably just some weird content mismatch, something going on like that. These are all, this is all part of the, what we need to evaluate to answer our questions. And that's really the last thing, I think we're out of questions and I'll, at a time, I guess we'll sort of leave everybody with this, that I think is a theme from this call is that it's easy. And I think a lot of people approach it this way and I'm not disparaging them in any way at all, but it's easy to look at a lot of what we do as client service professionals, especially with paid traffic in a reductionist linear way. Like let's go, let's look at this piece of data and try to figure out why this thing's not happening. And it's, in my experience, and the way you've heard Lauren describe it, it's never that, there's never a thing. It's always all the things and all the things tell you where to look. You know, your business is a system. It's a system with many parts and those parts are necessary, but individually they're not sufficient. And from the interaction of those parts, effects emerge that no single part produces. That's systems theory in like 90 words. So your business and your paid traffic and other things are an emergent effect of lots of things happening, the overwhelming majority of which you are completely unaware of. You have no idea the context of the person who is viewing your ad is in, how they feel, none of that. So so much of what we do is outside of our awareness. But we do have a body of information we can look at. Lauren talked about some of this when you was talking about how you might troubleshoot this. And it's not looking at, if you look just in your Facebook interface, you're going to see some data. And if you look at Google Analytics, you're going to see some data. And if you look at your email service provider, you're going to see some data. None of those pieces of information independently really will tell you anything unless there's just a technical mistake, right? Like a mistake like the pixel is not firing you've been getting of those 85 people, you got 25 sign ups, you just didn't know it. Like, okay, that's something that you might uncover and realize that the problem that you thought was happening wasn't the actual problem. There is no problem, everything's fine. In the absence of that, what we really need to do as client service professionals who work in this field is look at the system data and then infer from that data what we think the story is that's being told. We might have a certain audience or certain characteristics that have a certain set of metrics at the beginning of a journey. And those metrics then change when they interact with our content. And then there's a lot of other data, but monkeying around with the little pieces of that isn't going to produce much. In general, what it will do is it will move you backwards. And there's a sort of a funny thing that happens with the system is that when you try to make these linear reductionist changes, you often break the emergent effects you want from the system. So in a classic example, we use a lot. I think we've used it in some of the email series we've written is that if you have a system that has paid ads to generate leads to them, send people through an email series to buy a product, you can highly optimize lead gen and break the entire system. That you can make the acquisition of leads and do so many things to get so many more leads, but that actually has downstream consequences that kills the whole point of what you're doing. So it's critical that, and you don't have to spend 10 hours getting all the data and get paralyzed by the naval gazing, but you do need to know what the system is doing. And you need to know the part that you're concerned about. And Gareth's example that the party's concerned about is sending traffic to a landing page and not getting conversions. That exists within a larger context. So he needs to step back, we all do, always step back and look at the whole context, look at as many pieces of data as we can, and then tell ourselves the story that data is telling. And if we can't tell the story, and we need to keep at it until we can. And that story could be that you've had a thousand people who have interacted with this long ad and that ad has set them up to go to this webinar and of those 1,500 sign up and five show up. Well, something's wrong. We know now that we did one part really well, but there's a mismatch. And we don't care about what our click-through rate of the ad was when 500 out of 1,000 people are showing up to the webinar. Like that's misplaced precision. So then it would become a question of, how is this system I have created able to produce this weird result where we have 1,000 people click on an ad, 500 sign up for a webinar, five stay for the webinar. The system made a weird, and there's a weird emergent effect from that system of interaction. Let me look at the whole system and look at all of those parts and then decide what I think is going on, not look at what was my click-through rate or what is my opt-in rate. So those are just pieces of a larger puzzle. That's really the work. That's the work we spend our lifetime doing as professionals is we figure out what is contributing to the effects that we're seeing without breaking the system in the process. Lauren, Andre, Andre, you've been silent. I think you're kicking back, shopping for your racing. You're selling something for sale time. I think he's bought all the parts when we've been on this Q&A call. He's just like, whatever, he's got it all set up. Anything Lauren, Andre to add, I appreciate you guys always being on the call, of course. And thanks Lauren for all of your contributions. But as we wrap up, I want to say thanks to you guys as always, thanks for being here and anything you want to throw out there before we wrap up for the day. No, I'm good. Thanks everyone for your time and hit us up in the modules. We have any questions and I hope everyone goes out there and in honor of Sean, I hope everyone crushes it, bro. I think that one's coming. Yeah, this has been great. I don't have anything to add other than this was pretty awesome. Didn't even fall asleep. There's the standard of excellence we can always have. All right, everyone on the call and everyone who submitted questions, thank you very much. It's always a pleasure and an honor to be working with all of you. So as Lauren said, go forth and crush it. See you guys later. Yeah, it's pretty cool that 100% of people have stayed all the way through to the end. We can, fun with numbers. All right, see you guys later. Thank you, everyone.
